[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2017-10-20
[TITLE]: Halloween decorations taken down after being dubbed "racially offensive"
[METADATA/INTRO]: Halloween decorations taken down after being called "racially offensive" HANFORD, Calif. (KMPH) -- A homeowner in Hanford says she took down her Halloween decorations after Hanford Police told her they were "racially offensive."...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: "Anytime you have something black particularly simulating a body hanging from a tree is completely synonymous with lynching," said Reese. 
 The homeowner stuffed the bags and hung them up on a tree by the feet Sunday. The homeowner decided to take down the decorations after her conversation with officers. Despite being in the Halloween spirit Dr. Reese explained how some people could've viewed the decorations as inappropriate regardless the color of the plastic bags. 
 “In this case they were black bags. I think even if they would have been clear bags. Anytime I see a rope around a branch of a tree it only leads me to think about one thing. We have to be clear that who are the bodies historically hung from trees. It is not to say that other ethnic groups were never lynched because they were. But the great majority were people of African descent,” said Dr. Reese.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2014
[TITLE]: Tallahassee Democrat
[METADATA/INTRO]: Don’t bag up falling leaves Leaves are falling, and with this autumn season we are seeing bulging black vinyl bags springing up along the roadways to be picked up. The good news? There seems to be fewer black vinyl bags...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: It costs approximately 1.5 million dollars out of taxpayers' pockets to empty the contents of these black vinyl bags and chip up the debris at the Leon County Solid Waste Facility. Under new policy, black bags are emptied in front of the home and the empty bags are left in the yards of the homeowners for them to dispose. Unfortunately, these plastic bags usually end up in the landfill. A much better practice exists for everyone to use is as follows: 
 Don't rake leaves into black plastic bags. Rake leaves onto the bare soil under your trees or into your flower beds. Most homeowners have plenty of bare soil where nothing will grow. Putting your leaves onto the bare soil will provide the soil with carbon and nutrients, and prevent the all-too-prevalent risks of erosion — exposed tree roots. Exposed tree roots can eventually weaken and render a tree hazardous and prone to fall on unsuspecting people or cars during or after a storm.
